rationale: Thomas and Morwitz (2009) is cited in the introduction as key evidence in the conflicting-literature setup that motivates the paper's core research question about what numeric components to include in promotions. The paper returns to that study in the general discussion to interpret its own findings against the cited attenuation result and to propose boundary conditions for when extra discount information helps versus hurts. This is more than a passing mention because the cited study materially informs the framing and interpretation of the contribution.
relation_description: The citing paper uses Thomas and Morwitz (2009) as a central prior result on ease-of-computation in price difference judgments, then qualifies its implication that providing explicit discount differences can attenuate fluency effects. It argues and shows that in multi-number promotions, added discount information can improve processing and evaluations when the numbers are structurally related (approximation sequences or multiples).
